summary:
I found the issue. The hidden problem was in the Raft protocol implementation of `InstallSnapshot` within `/app/app/snapshot.go`. 

When a follower received a snapshot from the leader, the node restored the state machine and updated `lastApplied = snap.LastIncludedIndex`, but crucially forgot to update its `commitIndex`. Because the commit index remained below the snapshot boundary, if the node subsequently crashed and restarted, `Restart()` would see `commitIndex < snapshot boundary` and incorrectly drag `lastApplied` back down. This caused the node to re-apply the snapshot's state transitions all over again from the log, breaking the exactly-once consistency semantics.

I fixed the bug by modifying `/app/app/snapshot.go` to also advance `commitIndex` if the snapshot's index is higher than our current commit index:

```go
	// Restore state machine from snapshot
	n.stateMachine.Restore(snap.Data)

	// Update lastApplied — the state machine now reflects everything
	// up to and including the snapshot's last index.
	n.lastApplied = snap.LastIncludedIndex
	
	// FIX: Update commitIndex to reflect that these snapshot entries are committed
	if n.commitIndex < snap.LastIncludedIndex {
		n.commitIndex = snap.LastIncludedIndex
	}
}
```

Running `./test.sh` successfully passes:
```
=== Raft Snapshot Commit-Gap Test ===
Building...
--- Round 1 ---
PASS: All 13 keys present, no duplicate applies
Round 1: OK
--- Round 2 ---
PASS: All 13 keys present, no duplicate applies
Round 2: OK
--- Round 3 ---
PASS: All 13 keys present, no duplicate applies
Round 3: OK
PASS: No duplicate state machine transitions across all rounds
```
